anchor.snippet: A statistical hypothesis test is a method of statistical inference
any statement about the parameters describing a population can be a hypothesis
The test compares two hypotheses
If this probability (called the p-value) is less than the significance level of the test
A type I error , in which the null hypothesis is rejected despite the null hypothesis being true
A type II error , in which the null hypothesis is accepted despite the alternative hypothesis being true
is called the power of the test
Simple hypothesis : Any hypothesis which specifies the population distribution completely
Composite hypothesis: Any hypothesis which does not specify the population distribution completely
Positive data: Data that enable the investigator to reject a null hypothesis
Critical value s of a statistical test are the boundaries of the acceptance region of the test
The acceptance region is the set of values of the test statistic for which the null hypothesis is not rejected
Region of rejection / Critical region : The set of values of the test statistic for which the null hypothesis is rejected
Size : For simple hypotheses, this is the test's probability of incorrectly rejecting the null hypothesis
Statistical significance test : A predecessor to the statistical hypothesis test
Conservative test: A test is conservative if, when constructed for a given nominal significance level
Most powerful test: For a given size or significance level

anchor.snippet: The Neyman-Pearson decision rule is to reject the null hypothesis
applied to the radioactive suitcase example
When the null hypothesis is true and statistical assumptions are met, the probability that the p-value will be less than or equal to the significance level
The p -value is the probability that a test statistic which is at least as extreme as the one obtained would occur under the null hypothesis
then we say the null hypothesis is rejected at the chosen level of significance
When multiple true null hypothesis tests are conducted at once without adjustment
A bootstrap creates numerous simulated samples by randomly resampling (with replacement) the original, combined sample data
Arbuthnot examined birth records in London for each of the 82 years from 1629 to 1710
claimed to be able to tell whether the tea or the milk was added first to a cup
A person (the subject) is tested for clairvoyance
The Neyman–Pearson lemma of hypothesis testing says that a good criterion for the selection of hypotheses is the ratio of their probabilities
a test might be used to select among three hypotheses
